text: My name is Thomas Clancy.
I'm the associate chief of surgical oncology at Brigham and Women's Hospital in Boston and a cancer surgeon at Brigham and Women's in Danube Harbor.
Today we're going to be talking about a patient who underwent a robotic liver resection for colorectal cancer spread to the liver.
The patient had been treated for colorectal cancer and on routine surveillance was found to have an area of spread or metastasis to the right lobe of the liver.
Surgical resection was offered as part of her treatment course.
Based on her favorable anatomy, we felt that a minimally invasive approach would be warranted.
This patient was felt to be an ideal candidate for robotic hepatectomy.
The surgeon begins the robotic hepatectomy by mobilizing the liver, carefully detaching it from its anatomical attachments to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to separate areas of scar tissue in the liver, utilizing the wrist articulation capability of the robotic tools for precise manipulation.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully separate the liver from its posterior attachments, utilizing the enhanced articulation and control provided by the robotic system.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to visualize the tumor within the liver, providing real-time imaging to guide the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound device to visualize the tumor on the screen, providing precise imaging during the robotic hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound device to scan through the entire liver, ensuring no additional lesions are visible on the screen.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound device to identify a specific area on the liver, then scores or coagulates a line on the liver's surface with an energy device to mark the area for further resection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide through the liver tissue, ensuring a clear margin of normal healthy liver is obtained adjacent to the tumor for complete lesion removal.
The surgeon manipulates surgical instruments while the assistant uses a suction aspirator to clear fluids from the surgical field.
I have instruments that allow

The surgeon performs coagulation through the liver using an energy device, achieving hemostasis to minimize blood loss. Gentle retraction is applied to the liver, allowing for the grasping of vessels if bleeding occurs.
In this case, one advantage of the robotic technique is the three-dimensional visualization
One thing not appreciated on the video is the fact that the surgeon is able to appreciate true three-dimensional visualization on the camera due to the fact that essentially two cameras are used.
The surgeon rotates the liver upward to expose and dissect along the posterior border, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue planes in this area.
Visible clips are placed on the structures of the gallbladder following its removal, indicating previous ligation or securing of vessels.
as the gallbladder was essentially right in the way of this resection margin.
The surgeon continues the dissection through the liver, progressively separating tissue to remove the specimen.
The surgeon uses coagulation to cauterize the margin of the liver after the specimen has been removed, aiming to prevent any oozing from the resection site.
The surgeon covers the entire liver surface with a hemostatic agent, ensuring thorough application to prevent bleeding.
At the end, total blood loss was less than one hundred cc's and the specimen was removed through a port site or little opening just at the patient's umbilicus or belly button.
After a very brief hospital stay, the patient was discharged home and reported that after about five days, she was participating in full activities including driving and had really no pain at all.
She's able to resume other cancer treatments without any further delay.
